MACROECONOMIC: General economic indicators including Consumer Price Index inflation and Gross Domestic Product growth.
The Consumer Price Index for all urban consumers is published on a monthly basis.
Core inflation rate excludes the volatile food and energy components.
Annual headline inflation rate derived from the year-over-year CPI change.
Monthly percent change of the Consumer Price Index for transportation services.
The chained Consumer Price Index accounts for consumer substitution behavior.
Regional Consumer Price Index for the Midwest urban area is tracked quarterly.
Trimmed mean inflation rate computed by the exclusion of extreme price changes.
Median Consumer Price Index reflects the price change at the 50th percentile.
Food at home inflation index from the CPI food basket components.
Energy price index from the CPI includes gasoline, electricity, and natural gas.
Services inflation rate less energy services is reported as a distinct measure.
Commodities less food and energy price index is reported as core goods.
Producer Price Index for final demand serves as an upstream inflation signal.
Import price index measures the price change of goods purchased from abroad.
Personal Consumption Expenditures price index is the Federal Reserve's preferred gauge.
Inflation expectations five years ahead from the Survey of Consumer Expectations.
Break-even inflation rate derived from the yield spread between nominal and TIPS.
Sticky-price Consumer Price Index aggregates items with infrequent price changes.
Flexible-price CPI consists of items that adjust prices rapidly.
Seasonally adjusted Consumer Price Index removes calendar-related price effects.
Housing inflation component from the CPI shelter index includes rent and owners' equivalent rent.
Medical care services inflation rate from the CPI for urban consumers.
Apparel price index decline or increase is tracked by the Bureau of Labor Statistics.
Used cars and trucks price index is a volatile component of the CPI basket.
Inflation diffusion index measures the share of CPI categories with price increases.
Gross Domestic Product at market prices is reported quarterly by the national statistical agency.
Real GDP growth rate adjusts nominal output for inflation effects.
Nominal GDP measured in current prices reflects both output and price changes.
GDP per capita is computed as total GDP divided by the population.
Potential output estimated by the Congressional Budget Office serves as a benchmark.
Output gap computed as the percent deviation of actual GDP from potential GDP.
Gross National Product includes net income from abroad in addition to GDP.
Personal consumption expenditure component of GDP is tracked as a demand driver.
Gross private domestic investment in GDP includes business fixed investment and inventories.
Government consumption expenditures and gross investment are a GDP component.
Net exports of goods and services are subtracted from total domestic expenditure.
The industrial production index measures output for manufacturing, mining, and utilities.
Capacity utilization rate is reported by the Federal Reserve for the industrial sector.
Composite Index of Leading Economic Indicators aggregates ten forward-looking components.
Gross Domestic Income equals the sum of all income generated in production.
The manufacturing Purchasing Managers' Index from the ISM survey signals expansion or contraction.
Services sector PMI from the ISM non-manufacturing survey tracks business activity.
Retail sales less autos and gas are reported as a core consumption metric.
Durable goods orders for new manufactured goods serve as a proxy for business investment.
Construction spending total value is reported monthly by the Census Bureau.
New residential housing starts count the number of privately owned housing units begun.
Existing home sales volume is tracked by the National Association of Realtors.
Real GDP growth forecast from the Survey of Professional Forecasters.
Annual GDP benchmark revision is applied to incorporate comprehensive source data.
The GDP implicit price deflator is computed as the ratio of nominal to real GDP.
The civilian unemployment rate is reported monthly by the Bureau of Labor Statistics.
Labor force participation rate is the share of the population that is in the labor force.
The employment-population ratio counts the proportion of the working-age population with a job.
Number of unemployed persons measured by the household survey during the reference week.
U-6 unemployment rate includes discouraged workers and those marginally attached.
U-3 unemployment rate is the official headline measure of total unemployed as a percent of the labor force.
Average duration of unemployment spells in weeks is tracked from the CPS.
Median weeks unemployed for job losers and persons on temporary layoff.
Long-term unemployment share of the labor force unemployed for 27 weeks or more.
Job openings rate from the JOLTS survey counts unfilled positions at the end of the month.
The quits rate measures voluntary separations as a share of total employment.
The layoffs and discharges rate from the JOLTS report tracks involuntary separations.
Initial claims for unemployment insurance are filed weekly by state programs.
Continuing claims for unemployment insurance count individuals already in receipt of benefits.
The insured unemployment rate is the ratio of continuing claims to covered employment.
Total nonfarm payroll employment is reported from the establishment survey.
Private sector employment change less government workers is closely watched.
Manufacturing payrolls count of production and non-supervisory workers.
Temporary help services employment serves as an early indicator of labor demand.
Average hourly earnings of all private employees is reported in the jobs report.
Average weekly hours worked by production and nonsupervisory employees.
The labor force participation rate for prime-age workers aged 25 to 54.
Youth unemployment rate for persons aged 16 to 24 years.
Discouraged worker count: persons not in labor force who stopped active job search.
Wage growth tracker from the Federal Reserve Bank of Atlanta based on microdata.
The federal funds target rate set by the Federal Open Market Committee.
The federal funds effective rate is the volume-weighted median of overnight transactions.
The discount rate charged by the Federal Reserve for primary credit to banks.
The interest on reserve balances rate is paid by the Fed on bank reserves.
The overnight reverse repo rate sets a floor for money market rates.
The prime rate quoted by banks is typically three percentage points above the fed funds rate.
The one-year Treasury constant maturity rate is derived from the yield curve.
The yield on the ten-year Treasury note is a benchmark for long-term borrowing costs.
The mortgage rate for a thirty-year fixed-rate conforming loan is tracked weekly.
The Secured Overnight Financing Rate (SOFR) is a broad measure of overnight repo costs.
The European Central Bank deposit facility rate is set for euro area banks.
The Bank of England Bank Rate is the official monetary policy rate in the UK.
The People's Bank of China Loan Prime Rate serves as the benchmark for new loans.
The Bank of Japan policy interest rate on current account balances.
The monetary policy stance indicator reflects tightening or loosening by the central bank.
Forward guidance language from the central bank statement is parsed for policy bias.
Quantitative easing total asset purchase target in billions of currency units.
Quantitative tightening runoff pace for the central bank balance sheet.
The central bank balance sheet size is reported weekly with asset holdings.
Excess reserves held by depository institutions at the Federal Reserve.
The yield curve slope is computed as the spread between the 10-year and 2-year Treasury yields.
The term premium on long-term bonds is estimated from a dynamic model.
Inflation target adopted by the central bank as an explicit numerical objective.
Monetary policy uncertainty index derived from news coverage and market data.
